title: Structural Subtyping with Protocols

overview.what: Python's `typing.Protocol` allows you to define structural interfaces. Instead of checking if an object inherits from a specific class (nominal typing), Protocols check if an object *has the right methods and attributes* (structural typing). Think of a Protocol like a bouncer at a club who only checks if you have a valid ID card, not what family you belong to. For example, if you define a `Logger` Protocol with a `.log(message)` method, any class that implements `.log(message)` implicitly matches the Protocol, without needing to explicitly inherit from it. This gives you the safety of static typing with the flexibility of Python's natural duck typing.
overview.why: It decouples your code. You can specify what a function needs an object to *do* rather than what the object *is*, making it much easier to swap implementations or mock objects for testing without creating complex inheritance hierarchies.

why_learning_this: Writing functions that accept any object matching a shape, improving reusability
Mocking dependencies cleanly in unit tests without subclassing
Appeasing mypy when using Python's natural duck-typing patterns

common_mistakes.title: Inheriting from the Protocol unnecessarily
common_mistakes.explanation: You don't need to inherit from the Protocol class (e.g., `class MyLogger(LoggerProtocol):`). The whole point is that structural subtyping checks the shape implicitly. Just implement the methods.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting @runtime_checkable
common_mistakes.explanation: By default, Protocols are only for static type checkers like mypy. If you try to use `isinstance(obj, MyProtocol)` at runtime, it will crash unless you decorate the Protocol with `@typing.runtime_checkable`.

recall_questions.q: What is the difference between nominal typing (like regular inheritance) and structural typing (like Protocols)?
recall_questions.answer: Nominal typing checks the name of the class (what it inherits from), while structural typing checks the shape of the object (what methods and attributes it has).

recall_questions.q: Why would you use a Protocol instead of a standard base class?
recall_questions.answer: To decouple the implementation from the interface. It allows objects to satisfy type hints without explicitly inheriting from a common base class, which is especially useful for third-party classes or mocking.

understanding_checks.question: Will this code pass structural type checking?
understanding_checks.why: Because `DiskSaver` has a `save` method matching the signature required by the `Saver` protocol, it structurally matches even though it doesn't explicitly inherit from `Saver`.

understanding_checks.question: What happens if you use `isinstance(obj, MyProtocol)` without decorating `MyProtocol` with `@runtime_checkable`?
understanding_checks.answer: It will raise a TypeError at runtime.
understanding_checks.why: By default, Protocols are strictly for static type checking. Python cannot use them in `isinstance` checks at runtime unless explicitly marked with `@typing.runtime_checkable`.

practice_tasks.title: Define a Notifier Protocol
practice_tasks.prompt: Modify the starter code to define a `Notifier` Protocol with a `send(message: str) -> None` method. Then implement the `notify_all` function to iterate through the notifiers and call `send` on each one.
practice_tasks.starter_code:
from typing import Protocol

# Define Notifier Protocol here


def notify_all(notifiers: list[Notifier], message: str) -> None:
    pass

aha_moment.prediction: What does isinstance return, given that Duck does not inherit from Quacker?
aha_moment.common_guess: False, because Duck does not subclass Quacker.
aha_moment.why: It prints True. Because Quacker is a runtime_checkable Protocol, isinstance() checks if donal has a quack() method, not its class hierarchy.

challenge.title: A Generic Storage Protocol
challenge.prompt: You are building an app that can save data to multiple places (e.g. S3, local disk, database). Define a `Storage` protocol that requires a `save(filename: str, data: str) -> bool` method. Then, write a concrete class `LocalDiskStorage` that implements this shape (it can just print 'Saving {data} to {filename}' and return True). Finally, write a function `backup_data(storage: Storage)` that uses the protocol.
